items.correct: The process by which wealthier residents move into a previously low-income urban area, raising property prices and often displacing original residents.
items.misconception: Gentrification is when the government rebuilds an old part of the city.
items.random_wrong: Gentrification means the city becomes more polluted as it grows.
items.why: Students confuse gentrification (private market-led displacement) with regeneration (planned redevelopment). The key feature is who benefits and who is pushed out.

items.q: What is the main social impact of gentrification?
items.correct: Displacement of long-term lower-income residents as rents and property prices rise, often along with loss of community businesses and cultural identity — even if the area looks 'improved'.
items.misconception: Gentrification only has positive effects because it makes areas nicer.
items.random_wrong: Gentrification means evicting all residents by force.
items.why: Improvements aren't free — they're paid for by displacement. Students focus on visible upgrades and miss the demographic shift. Many evaluation questions ask 'for whom is gentrification beneficial?'.

items.q: Why do informal settlements develop in rapidly growing LIC cities?
items.correct: Rural-urban migration outpaces formal housing supply; migrants build their own homes on marginal land without secure land tenure — driven by housing shortage, not by personal choice.
items.misconception: People live in slums because they prefer them or are too lazy to find better housing.
items.random_wrong: Informal settlements are built by the government for new migrants.
items.why: Self-construction is a response to formal-sector failure. Many residents are employed and pay informal rent. Students moralise; the geographical analysis is about supply, tenure and land economics.
It's 700000. “70000” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Rising rents and displacement. “Falling property values” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: London life expectancy gap between richest and poorest boroughs (years):
items.why: Exact answer: 9.5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
